points.text: Introduce JWT generation alongside existing session cookies; keep both auth methods enabled during rollout.
Store only minimal data (user_id, roles) in JWT; avoid sensitive or large payloads.
Use short JWT expiration (15‑30 min) with refresh tokens to limit damage from token theft.
Sign JWTs with strong algorithm (HS256 or RS256) and rotate signing keys regularly.
Validate JWTs before any DB access; reject missing/expired claims to reduce DB load.
Add rate‑limiting and circuit‑breaker around DB calls to mitigate 5xx spikes during migration.
Monitor error rates, latency, and token validation failures; be ready to revert to cookies if thresholds breach.
Do not store JWTs in insecure client storage; prefer HttpOnly secure cookies for token delivery.
Implement token revocation strategy (blacklist or short TTL) since JWTs cannot be invalidated otherwise.
Maintain CSRF protections for cookie‑based auth; JWTs in Authorization header are not CSRF‑vulnerable.
Run a phased rollout: keep session‑cookie auth as fallback while gradually issuing JWTs to a subset of users.
Issue short‑lived access JWTs (e.g., 5‑15 min) and store long‑lived refresh tokens in httpOnly, Secure cookies.
Validate JWT signature, exp, nbf, iss, aud on every request; reject tokens failing any check.
Never place PII or secrets in JWT payload; keep claims minimal and non‑sensitive.
Rotate signing keys regularly; include a kid header so old tokens can be verified during transition.
Enforce HTTPS everywhere; set Secure and SameSite=strict on any cookies used for refresh tokens.
Implement a revocation strategy: short TTLs, token blacklist, or rotate keys to invalidate compromised tokens.
Deploy canary releases and monitor 5xx rates; roll back immediately if error spikes exceed thresholds.
Add rate limiting and circuit‑breaker logic to protect DB during peak traffic and reduce contention.
Use connection pooling and async query handling to mitigate DB contention while the JWT system warms up.
